fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we've been getting complaints from a few internal devs that our numeric safety toolkit is hard to use consistently — people keep accidentally doing things like mixing up index types with plain integers, or forgetting to check if an optional value is actually there before using it. We also had a bug last sprint where someone did a cast without realizing data was getting silently truncated (you know, the same kind of thing we ran into with that old unit conversion module). There's also been confusion around the flag management stuff — devs aren't sure whether toggle returns the old state or the new one, and whether the conditional set operations actually report back what happened. Oh and the interval boundary behavior tripped someone up — they weren't sure if the endpoints counted or not. We want a single adapter binary that reads a JSON command from stdin and writes a clean key=value report to stdout, covering all these scenarios. Output needs to be totally deterministic and byte-comparable. Boolean values must render as lowercase true/false in the report lines. Numbers should just print naturally. Errors should report a normalized category, not raw exception text. Can we get this specced out properly and implemented? Should cover integers, floats, booleans, flags, index math, constraints, optionals, variants, and the compact storage trick where the empty state lives inside the value domain itself.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: int main() — reads one JSON object from stdin, dispatches on field 'fn', writes key=value lines to stdout

trigger_keywords: post increment return value
what does post_increment give back
pre vs post increment result
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Post-increment returns the value BEFORE the increment (the prior value), and the stored state becomes value+1. Pre-increment returns the UPDATED value. Both must be reported: result= is what the operator returned, value= is the state after the operation. For example, post_increment on 7 yields result=7 and value=8.

trigger_keywords: toggle returns what
does toggle give old state or new state
flag toggle return behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: toggle returns the PRIOR state (the state before toggling), not the new state. The output lines are returned= (what toggle gave back) and state= (the resulting state). So toggling a flag that starts false yields returned=false and state=true.

trigger_keywords: try_set try_reset return value
conditional set reports changed
did try_set actually change anything
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: try_set succeeds and returns true only if the flag was NOT already set (i.e., it actually changed). try_reset succeeds and returns true only if the flag WAS set and got cleared. Both report returned= (whether a change happened) and state= (the resulting flag state).

trigger_keywords: interval endpoint included
are bounds closed or open
lower_closed upper_closed meaning
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each bound is independently configurable. When lower_closed=true the lower endpoint IS included in the interval (value >= lower). When lower_closed=false the lower endpoint is excluded (value > lower). Same logic applies to upper_closed for the upper bound. All four combinations are valid.

trigger_keywords: error output format constraint violation
how to report a constraint error
constraint violation output lines
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a constraint is violated the output must be exactly two lines: 'error=constraint_violation' followed by 'constraint=<name>' where <name> is the normalized constraint identifier (e.g. non_null, non_empty, non_default). No exception type names, no stack traces, no extra fields.

trigger_keywords: boolean stream rendering
bool written as 1 or true
boolean io output format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The boolean wrapper renders as 1 or 0 on a stream (written= line uses 1/0), but the logical state is reported as true/false in key=value report lines (read= line uses true/false). The io scenario reports written= as the streamed integer form and read= as the parsed logical form.

trigger_keywords: index advanced by distance type
can index add plain integer
index arithmetic operand type
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An Index type can only be advanced by a separate signed Distance type — not by a raw integer. The prev helper on an index moves backward by the given distance value (i.e., subtracts it). So starting at 5, += 3 gives 8, then prev by 2 gives index=6.

trigger_keywords: compact optional empty state encoding
space optimized optional policy
compact_optional bool sentinel NaN policy
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The compact optional encodes its empty state inside the value domain itself depending on the policy: 'bool' policy uses a boolean sentinel, 'int' policy uses a sentinel integer value, 'float' policy uses NaN. The lifecycle always reports initial_has_value=false, then after emplacing: has_value=true and value=<v>, then after destroy: after_destroy_has_value=false.

trigger_keywords: overflow detection which integer size
overflow_check signed int width
what type does overflow_check use
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Overflow and underflow detection operates on 32-bit signed or unsigned integers (int32_t / uint32_t). The predicate checks BEFORE the operation whether it would overflow, underflow, or be undefined (including division/modulo by zero), and reports will_error=true or will_error=false.

trigger_keywords: literal parser digit separator
constant_parser hex octal binary
numeric literal token formats supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The compile-time literal parser handles decimal, hexadecimal (0x prefix), octal (0 prefix), and binary (0b prefix) bases, plus digit separators (apostrophe ' character). For example token '23\'900' parses to 23900. Output is value=<n> with the parsed integer value.

trigger_keywords: deferred construction initial state
lazy init slot starts empty
deferred slot has_value before assign
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A deferred construction slot starts with no value. The output reports initial_has_value=false before assignment, then after assignment reports has_value=true and value=<n>. The slot can be filled via direct assignment or in-place construction and must only be filled once.

trigger_keywords: output parameter write through
output_parameter assign_value op
backing storage after output param assign
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The output parameter writes through to backing storage on assignment. The assign_value op sets the backing store to the given value. The report line is backing=<value>. For string values the backing stores the string as-is.

trigger_keywords: strong typedef operator set
strong typedef arithmetic ops available
which operators does strong typedef support
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The strong typedef opts into arithmetic, comparison, and bitwise operator sets. Supported arithmetic ops include +=, -=, *=, /=, %=, and their binary forms (+, -, *, /, %). It remains a distinct type that cannot be implicitly mixed with the underlying primitive.

trigger_keywords: tagged union type index value
tagged_union emplace type_index output
which type index for int vs double in tagged union
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: After emplacing an int alternative the type_index reported is 1. The lifecycle output is: initial_has_value=false, then type_index=<n> and value=<v> after emplace, then after_destroy_has_value=false after destroy. The type index corresponds to the position of the alternative in the union definition (int=1, double=2, or similar).

trigger_keywords: flag_set how many flags
flag set named flags a b c
flag_set all query meaning
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The flag set has exactly three named flags: a, b, and c. The all= query is true only when all three are set; none= is true only when none are set; any= is true when at least one is set. Individual membership is reported as a=, b=, c= boolean lines in that order.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'post_increment', 'io', 'addition', 'toggle', 'assign_value'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same kind of thing we ran into with that old unit conversion module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 7 (narrow_cast): explicit narrowing conversion between integer widths or floating-point widths — lossy silent truncation is the historical problem being referenced, handled via the narrow_cast fn with kind=integer or kind=float

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 16 (constrained_throwing / constraint enforcement): the non_null, non_empty, non_default predicate checks and their normalized error output format (error=constraint_violation + constraint=<name>), mirroring a pattern presumably used in a prior auth/login feature

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compact storage trick where the empty state lives inside the value domain itself
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 26 (compact_optional): space-optimized optional whose empty state is encoded within the value domain using a policy — bool sentinel, integer sentinel, or NaN float — dispatched via policy field in the compact_optional fn

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented right now — could you check the test case JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the relevant feature, or look at the expected_output fields there? Those are the authoritative contract for output format and behavior.

feature01_integer_arithmetic.json.description: Safe wrapper over a built-in integer: a sequence of compound and binary arithmetic operations (including reversed operands) yields the final stored value.

feature02_integer_unary_incdec.json.description: Unary plus/minus and pre/post increment & decrement on the integer wrapper, reporting both the returned value and the resulting state.

feature03_integer_comparison.json.description: All six relational comparisons between two integer wrappers.

cases.expected_output:
eq=false
ne=true
lt=true
le=true
gt=false
ge=false

feature04_integer_conversion_io.json.description: Signed/unsigned conversions, absolute value, and stream round-trip of the integer wrapper.

feature05_floating_point.json.description: Floating-point wrapper arithmetic sequences and ordered comparison.

feature06_boolean.json.description: Boolean wrapper: logical negation, equality/inequality against a plain bool, and stream output/input round-trip (rendered as 1/0).

feature07_narrow_cast.json.description: Explicit narrowing conversion between integer widths and between floating-point widths.

feature08_overflow_detection.json.description: Predicate detection of whether a signed/unsigned arithmetic operation would overflow or underflow before it is performed.

feature09_flag.json.description: Two-state flag with deliberate transitions: toggle, change, set/reset and the conditional try_set/try_reset that report whether they changed the state.

feature10_flag_set.json.description: Fixed-width set of named flags supporting set/reset/toggle of individual and all flags plus bitwise composition, reporting membership and aggregate any/none/all queries.

cases.expected_output:
a=true
b=false
c=true
any=true
none=false
all=false

feature11_index_arithmetic.json.description: Index type advanced by a separate signed distance type via +=, -=, binary +/-, and the next/prev/advance helpers.

feature12_index_distance_at.json.description: Signed distance between two indices and type-safe element access into a container by index.

feature13_relational_constraints.json.description: Single-bound relational constraint predicates (less, less-or-equal, greater, greater-or-equal) evaluated against a batch of queries.

feature14_interval_constraints.json.description: Interval membership constraint with each bound independently open or closed.

feature15_clamping.json.description: Clamping verifier that forces a value into a closed interval, and the make_clamped / make_bounded constructors that report the stored value and the recorded bounds.

feature16_constraint_enforcement.json.description: Enforcing a constraint at assignment time: a non-null constrained value rejects a null assignment by raising a normalized constraint violation, while predefined predicates (non-null, non-empty, non-default) report satisfaction.

feature17_literal_parser.json.description: Compile-time numeric literal parser exposed for a catalogue of tokens covering decimal, hexadecimal, octal, binary bases and digit separators.

feature18_downcast.json.description: Safe downcast from a base reference to a derived reference that preserves object identity.

feature19_strong_typedef.json.description: Strong typedef over a primitive that opts into arithmetic, comparison and bitwise operator sets while remaining a distinct type.

feature20_deferred_construction.json.description: A storage slot that starts empty and is later filled exactly once via assignment or in-place construction.

feature21_output_parameter.json.description: An output parameter that writes through to backing storage via assignment, repeated-fill assignment, or repeated writes.

feature22_optional.json.description: Optional value: presence test, bool conversion, value_or fallback, the map transform applied only when engaged, and full comparison against another optional that may be empty.

feature23_optional_ref.json.description: Optional reference that either refers to an existing object or to nothing, with a value_or fallback.

feature24_variant.json.description: Tagged variant over several alternatives: active-alternative queries, value_or, comparison against a value, and exception safety under different empty-state policies.

feature25_tagged_union.json.description: Low-level tagged union: emplace an alternative, report the active type index and value, then destroy and confirm it is empty.

feature26_compact_optional.json.description: Space-optimized optional storage whose empty state is encoded in the value domain itself (boolean, sentinel integer, NaN float): lifecycle of create/destroy.
